# Declared, Exemplified, Guided, Governed

## Statement

Every governed thing in a JOS-led venture must climb a 4-step ladder
— **Declared, Exemplified, Guided, Governed** — in that order:

1. **Declared** — the thing's existence is named, located, and
   identifiable in the navigation layer
2. **Exemplified** — there is a canonical instance, template, or
   worked example showing what "good" looks like
3. **Guided** — there is documentation, a playbook, or embedded
   guidance telling readers how to engage with the thing
4. **Governed** — there is a binding rule, contract, or standing
   order enforcing the commitment about the thing

The order is non-negotiable. You cannot exemplify what hasn't been
declared. You cannot guide engagement with what isn't exemplified.
You cannot govern adherence to what isn't guided. The ladder ascends.

## Rationale

JOS exists to make the meta-layer — the way ventures organize
themselves — uniform enough that agents and humans can navigate the
fleet without per-venture guesswork. That uniformity requires every
governed thing to be reachable through a predictable sequence of
discoveries: I find it (declaration), I see what good looks like
(exemplification), I learn how to engage (guidance), I am bound to
the commitment (governance).

Each rung serves a distinct audience and need:

| Rung | Question it answers | Audience |
|------|---------------------|----------|
| Declared | "Does this thing exist?" | Discovery agents, new contributors |
| Exemplified | "What does a good instance look like?" | Builders, copy-pattern learners |
| Guided | "How do I engage with it correctly?" | Operators, day-to-day users |
| Governed | "What am I bound to?" | Compliance, auditors, gate machinery |

Skipping rungs produces predictable failure modes:

- **Skip Declared** → undeclared things are dark space. Agents miss
  them, governance can't check them, assembly can't depend on them.
- **Skip Exemplified** → new instances are written from scratch each
  time, diverge, and the "type" becomes meaningless.
- **Skip Guided** → readers know the thing exists and can copy a
  template but don't know the spirit of correct use. Surface compliance
  passes; substantive compliance fails.
- **Skip Governed** → guidance is aspirational. The right thing is
  the easiest thing only when there is also a constraint keeping the
  wrong thing from being easier.

## How JOS already practices this (existing examples)

The principle is not a new policy — it codifies a pattern JOS already
follows when it does governance well. Examples:

| Thing | Declared | Exemplified | Guided | Governed |
|-------|----------|-------------|--------|----------|
| **Charter** | `distribution/charters/*.yaml` registered in registry | `distribution/charters/_template.yaml` | JOS-SPEC-001 spec + JOS-PB-007 onboarding playbook | JOS-K05 charter schema contract, JOS-GATE-frontmatter |
| **Context bundle** | Declared in charter's `venture_config.context_bundle_path` | `instruments/templates/new-context-bundle/` | JOS-PB-007 onboarding framework | JOS-R06 + JOS-K04 (completeness contract) |
| **CLAUDE.md** | Declared per JOS-R18 | `instruments/kits/venture-birthing-pack/CLAUDE.md.template` | JOS-PB-006 Agent Behavioral Standards | JOS-R18 + JOS-GATE-009 |
| **Library atom** | Registered in `libraries/atoms/_index.yaml` | Existing atoms as worked examples | JOS-SOP-010 authoring SOP | JOS-D0050 features standard |
| **Layer** | `architecture/layers/_index.yaml` | The 3 instances (Knowledge, Governance, Action) | The 9 README/CONTRACT/ARCHITECTURE docs | JOS-D0053 |

Things JOS currently has half-up the ladder — and which JOSW2 scope
670 will complete:

| Thing | Status |
|-------|--------|
| OVERVIEW.md as a doc-type | Not declared as a JOS object type (inbox proposal); no exemplar; no guidance; no governance. **Bottom of ladder.** |
| VISION.md as a doc-type | Examples exist (JOS itself, Foundry) but no formal type declaration, no section schema, no governance |
| WDGLL.md | Methodology declared (JOS-PB-002) but the *doc-type* itself isn't declared with a schema |
| Per-system HEALTH-STANDARD | Foundry pattern exists; not JOS-declared |
| `_manifest.yaml` for discoverable products/systems/subsystems | Not yet declared at all (Phase 4 of scope 670 produces this) |

The pattern made operational: scope 670 walks every doc-type and
declared-thing in the fleet up the ladder, in order, until the
declaration surface is uniform.

## What this principle is NOT

- **Not a rejection of work-in-progress.** A thing climbing the
  ladder is governed, but a thing on rung 1 (Declared, not yet
  Exemplified) is still legitimately part of the system. The
  principle is about the *destination*, not a quality gate at every
  intermediate state.
- **Not equally applied to every object type.** The matrix produced
  by JOSW2 scope 670 Phase 3 specifies which rungs are required for
  which object types. A single-purpose script needs only Declared +
  Governed (its registry entry); a Product needs all four.
- **Not a substitute for judgment about WHICH things should exist.**
  The principle governs HOW a thing becomes fully governable, not
  WHETHER it should exist in the first place.

## Connected Objects

- **JOS-C45** — Declaration Precedes Governance (the underlying physics)
- **JOS-SO-015** — Declaration Required for Governable Things (the operational rule)
- **JOS-FW-001** — Holonic Decomposition Framework (each holonic level climbs the ladder independently)
- **JOS-D0049** — Universal Base Manifest (declaration cascade)
